name: Mill Rush — Parallel

branch_moves.name: Mill Rush Parallel — d1 Variant
branch_moves.strategic_notes: W plays d1 to contest the south cross-line instead of sealing line 2.

strategic_notes: W builds toward the b2-d2-f2 mill (line 2) and the f2-f4-f6 mill (line f). B mirrors with b6-d6-f6 (line 6), forcing parallel horizontal mills. W must fill b2 on ply 7 to close line 2; leaving it open risks B claiming it.
source_reference: Chapter 15.2, Game 1a — parallel lines mill rush

name: Mill Rush — Perpendicular
strategic_notes: B does not claim f6, allowing W to form the f2-f4-f6 mill (vertical). W places d1 as piece 8 to contest the south cross-line and block B's potential b2-d2 mill. The two mills (line f vertical, line 2 horizontal) are perpendicular. W is more mobile than in the parallel setup.
source_reference: Chapter 15.2, Game 2a — perpendicular lines mill rush

name: Mill Rush — Parallel (Alt)
strategic_notes: B fills b2 to block W's line 2 mill. B gains potential b2-b4-b6. W should play d1 then redirect toward outer ring mills.
source_reference: Chapter 15.2, Game 1b — parallel lines (alt) mill rush

name: Mill Rush — Perpendicular (Alt)
strategic_notes: B fills b2 in the perpendicular setup, blocking W's line 2 and forming potential b2-b4-b6. W retains f2-f4-f6 vertical mill. B rarely does this as it costs tempo.
source_reference: Chapter 15.2, Game 2b — perpendicular lines (alt) mill rush

name: Mill Rush — Extended Parallel
strategic_notes: W extends into the outer ring after sealing line 2, building toward a7-d7-g7 outer top mill. This is usually a stalemate if played correctly.
source_reference: Chapter 15.2, Game 3 — extended parallel mill rush

name: Inverted Mill Rush — Parallel
strategic_notes: W starts at d7 (inverted) then builds f6-f4-f2 (line f, vertical). B mirrors on line b. After both sides form horizontal mills (line 6 for W, line 2 for B) the game resembles the standard parallel mill rush but mirrored. Piece 15 placement at c4 is critical.
source_reference: Chapter 15.3 — Inverted Mill Rush, parallel lines

name: Inverted Mill Rush — Perpendicular
strategic_notes: W builds f2-f4-f6 vertically; B is forced into perpendicular layout. W's c4 piece contests the west cross-line (a4-b4-c4) and restricts B. If W does not place c4, B can take it and dominate the inner square.

name: Battle Lines
strategic_notes: W builds multiple potential mills aggressively across all ring sections. B responds by mirroring. The game rewards the side that converts potential mills first — W usually has more options if it keeps removing B pieces strategically and does not allow B to isolate its pieces.

name: Z Mill — Closed

branch_moves.name: Z Mill — c4/e3 Variant
branch_moves.strategic_notes: B plays e5 to threaten inner-ring mill c5-d5-e5.

strategic_notes: W builds c4 and e3 to create a Z-shaped arrangement of potential mills across the inner ring: c3-c4-c5 (left), e3-d3-c3 (bottom), and d1-d2-d3 (south cross). B must contest the inner ring to prevent W from dominating it. The Z shape creates cross-ring threats that are hard to block simultaneously.

name: Z Mill — Open
strategic_notes: W first claims d2 and f4 (middle ring), then pivots to the inner ring with c4 and e3. This variation leaves the f2-f4-f6 mill potential partially open while threatening inner-ring control. B should contest d5 or e5 to prevent W from sealing the inner ring.

name: Corner Gambit
strategic_notes: W occupies all four outer ring corners early. Each pair threatens a potential mill along the outer ring sides. B must respond to contain the outer ring. This can be a gambit trap — the predictability of needing to block open mills can help W plan traps.

name: Z Mill c4/e3 — Ring Round the Rosie
Z Mill c4/e3 — All Along the Watchtower
Z Mill c4/e3 — Cut the Bottom Out
Z Mill c4/e3 — Roof Falls In
